essential_question: Can you measure a real angle in the world, and then draw one yourself to an exact degree?
objective: Measure real-world angles with a protractor to the nearest whole degree, and draw and label a set of target angles (30°, 90°, 135°) accurately.

agenda.title: Brief — From Protractor Basics to Real Angles

content.heading: From reading a scale to owning it
bullets: Yesterday: named angles by vertex & sides, and learned to read a protractor.
Today: hunt three real angles in your world, measure them, then draw a set of exact target angles.
The test: can you measure to the nearest degree — and hit a target angle within a couple degrees when you draw?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather your protractor, ruler, pencil, and paper. Decide which three real angles you'll hunt (a door ajar, a clock's hands, a ramp, a laptop screen, open scissors) before you start measuring.

content.heading: Measure the world, then draw to spec
bullets: Angle Hunt — find and measure three real angles; record what each object was and its measured degree.
Target Draw — using your protractor, draw and label three angles at exactly 30°, 90°, and 135°.
For each drawn angle, mark the vertex, both rays, and write m∠ = its value beside it.
Re-check every drawn angle with the protractor before you call it done.

content.preview: Session 3: defend why an angle's measure never depends on how long you draw its rays, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can measure a real-world angle with a protractor to the nearest whole degree
I can draw an angle of a given measure accurately using a protractor
I can label a drawn angle's vertex and rays and write its measure correctly, and check my own accuracy

questions.prompt: List the three real-world angles you hunted (what each object was, and which two rays you measured) and the degree you measured for each, to the nearest whole degree.
questions.answer: Any three honest, plausible real objects with degree measures, e.g. door ajar ≈ 62°, clock hands at 3:00 = 90°, ramp ≈ 15°.
questions.explain: Rubric: three named real objects with their vertex/sides described (1 pt) + three degree measures each to the nearest whole degree (1 pt).

questions.prompt: On the grid, use a protractor to draw and label three separate angles at exactly m∠ = 30°, 90°, and 135°, marking each vertex.
questions.answer: Three angles drawn from labeled vertices, each opening to its target measure (30°, 90°, 135°), each labeled with its m∠ value.
questions.explain: Rubric: all three vertices and both rays clearly drawn (1 pt) + all three measures within a degree or two of target when checked with a protractor (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Pick one of your drawn angles and explain how you checked it was accurate — what exactly did you do with the protractor after drawing it?
questions.answer: Laid the protractor's center back on the vertex, aligned 0° with one ray, and read where the other ray crossed the scale to confirm it landed within a degree or two of the target.
questions.explain: Full credit: describes re-aligning the protractor on the vertex and baseline ray, then reading the second ray's position on the correct scale.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which was harder — measuring a real angle in the world, or drawing one to an exact degree? What did the hands-on hunt and draw teach you that the definitions alone didn't?
